title: Musings on Markets
gist: Damodaran argues the fight over whether SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic should enter the S&P 500 is driven by self-serving agendas of active managers, academics and politicians, and that inclusion will barely move these stocks' prices because the index-inclusion bump has faded to near zero. He uses the debate to mount a broader defense of passive investing, dismantling the 'passive feeds momentum,' 'hidden risk to retail,' and 'money-losing equals bad investment' arguments.

themes.theme: Who opposes index inclusion and why

insights.claim: Opposition to including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in the S&P 500 comes mainly from three self-interested groups: active managers losing the war against passive funds, academics claiming to protect retail investors, and politicians attacking billionaires.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discount the 'protect investors' and 'market efficiency' framing in the inclusion debate as motivated reasoning; weigh the underlying mechanics instead.
insights.quote: I see a combination of hidden agendas and misguided views about investing behind each of the three groups that are most vehemently against inclusions.
insights.locator: I am skeptical, since I see a combination

insights.claim: Active managers frame likely index inclusion of mega-cap money-losers as an added risk to passive investing that can supposedly be avoided by paying active managers to sidestep it.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat marketing that reframes index risk as a reason to pay active fees as a sales tactic, not analysis.
insights.quote: present this as an added risk to passive investing that can be avoided by paying these professional money managers to avoid that risk
insights.locator: First, you have a cadre of active investors

insights.claim: S&P 500 inclusion requires being listed at least a year, having sufficient free-float liquidity, and positive profits in the four quarters leading into listing.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: These three gating rules are exactly what bars money-losing newly-public mega-caps from immediate inclusion.
insights.quote: caveats on inclusion relating to listing age (listed at least a year), liquidity (measured by looking at shares that are available for investors to trade in the market, i.e., free float) and profitability (positive profits in the four quarters leading into the listing)
insights.locator: Constituents: The first and perhaps most critical

insights.claim: Price-weighting, as used by the Dow 30, has no redeeming analytical quality and breaks down right after stock splits.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat Dow 30 moves as a poor proxy for market performance versus cap-weighted measures.
insights.quote: I cannot think of a single redeeming quality to price weighting, since it measures very little of consequence, and suffers from breakdowns, right after stock splits.
insights.locator: Weights:  You can have two indices

insights.claim: Because the S&P 500 weights by free float, Meta is counted below its true weight (Zuckerberg's class B voting shares are excluded) and Walmart is similarly understated due to family-controlled holdings.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Float-adjustment means founder/family-controlled stocks carry less index influence than their headline market cap implies.
insights.quote: Meta punches in below its true weight, since the bulk of class B shares (which are voting shares held by Zuckerberg) are not counted, as does Walmart, where some family-controlled holdings are treated as non-traded
insights.locator: The third and most widely used mechanism

insights.claim: On June 15, 2026 the S&P 500 closed at 7554.29 against a float-adjusted market cap of $63,498.44 billion, implying index units of 0.1190 (index level divided by float-adjusted market cap).
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The index-units mechanic is what lets a trillion-dollar addition enter without jolting the index level.
insights.quote: Index units = Index level / Float-adjusted market capitalization = 7554.29/ 63498.44 = 0.1190
insights.locator: Consider, for instance, the S&P 500 which ended trading

insights.claim: Aggregated dollar dividends on the S&P 500 were $664.90 billion in 2025, which times the 0.1190 index units yields an index dividend of 79.12 for the year.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Adding money-losing mega-caps mechanically dilutes index earnings and dividend fundamentals.
insights.quote: the aggregated dollar dividends on the S&P 500 was $664.90 billion, and multiplying that value by the index units (0.1190) yields an index dividend of 79.12 for the year
insights.locator: In 2025, for instance, the aggregated dollar dividends

insights.claim: When a company is swapped into an index, the index level is held steady by adjusting the index units, so a trillion-dollar entrant replacing a one-billion-dollar exit leaves the level unchanged but lowers index units (in the example from 0.1190 to 0.1171).
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Inclusion is a non-event for the index level on day one but reshapes its forward fundamentals.
insights.quote: Note that while the index level will be unchanged by the addition of the trillion dollar company, the other components that it brings with it, including higher growth and perhaps negative earnings, will alter the fundamentals of the index going forward.
insights.locator: Index changes (inclusions and exclusions)

insights.claim: Including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in the S&P 500 would leave the index level unchanged at the moment of replacement but bring more risk, a near-term hit to earnings, and possibly higher long-term growth.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Passive S&P 500 holders should expect a changed risk/earnings profile, not an index jump, if these names are added.
insights.quote: it will change the index fundamentally going forward, bringing in more risk, a near term hit to earnings and perhaps a long term increase in growth
insights.locator: With these index mechanics in mind

themes.theme: S&P 500 as benchmark and the SPIVA verdict

insights.claim: The S&P 500's 500 names represent over 80% of the market cap of nearly 6000 publicly traded US equities, making it the single best proxy for aggregate US equity performance.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For aggregate-market exposure the cap-weighted S&P 500 is hard to beat; use equal-weight only to gauge the average stock.
insights.quote: the index represents more than 80% of the market capitalization of all US equities
insights.locator: The reason that the S&P 500 has acquired primacy

insights.claim: Over the last twenty-five years there have been only three years in which more than 50% of active large-cap funds beat the S&P 500, and only barely.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The base rate for large-cap active outperformance is so low that paying for it is rarely justified.
insights.quote: Over the last twenty five years, there have been three years where more than 50% of active funds have beaten the S&P 500 index, and barely so
insights.locator: Over the last twenty five years

insights.claim: SPIVA and Morningstar broke the active-management excuse by comparing fund returns to style-matched index funds, and across every style category no fund group outperformed its respective index over the past ten years.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Style-adjusted data removes the 'wrong benchmark' defense, strengthening the case for indexing in every category.
insights.quote: there is not a single fund group in any style that outperforms its respective index past ten years
insights.locator: There is very little hope in these numbers

insights.claim: As private equity and hedge funds have grown larger, their performance has come to resemble mutual funds, eroding the two-decade-old belief that they could systematically beat the market.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Endowment and pension allocations to alternatives chasing alpha face the same scale-driven mediocrity as mutual funds.
insights.quote: as they have become larger, they have come to resemble mutual funds, in terms of performance
insights.locator: Thus, two decades ago, the notion that private equity

insights.claim: Vanguard, which listed an S&P 500 index fund in 1976, now offers more than a hundred ETFs and more than two hundred index funds.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Product proliferation lets investors index almost any market or subset, deepening passive's structural advantage.
insights.quote: Vanguard, a pioneer when it listed the S&P 500 index fund in 1976, now lists more than a hundred ETFs and more than two hundred index funds
insights.locator: Today, you can not only invest in index funds

insights.claim: The fees the index-fund business generates create an incentive for index creators to favor a poorly constructed index that investors love to trade over a better-constructed but less attractive one.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Scrutinize index methodology; commercial incentives can degrade construction quality in popular products.
insights.quote: it is possible and perhaps even likely that the fund creators will pick the former
insights.locator: The size of the index fund business

insights.claim: The price bump from being added to the S&P 500 (and the drop from removal) has shrunk and become more transitory over time, and in the last decade or two has largely disappeared.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Do not trade index inclusion or exclusion as a durable price catalyst; the edge is gone.
insights.quote: bump in stock prices from being included in the index has become smaller and more transitory over time, and in the last decade or two decades, it has largely disappeared
insights.locator: Looking across these studies, there seems to be a consensus

insights.claim: An S&P study of 715 additions and 711 deletions to the S&P 500 between January 1995 and June 2021 showed the positive and negative excess-return effects of index changes depleting over time.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Robust large-sample evidence backs fading inclusion effects, undercutting the 'passive flows lift included stocks' thesis.
insights.quote: S&P took a look at the 715 companies added and 711 company deletions made to the S&P 500 between January 1995 and June 2021
insights.locator: To back this up, I look at one of the most complete studies

insights.claim: Despite surging assets indexed to the S&P 500, the effect of being added or removed has become smaller, not larger, and added companies are now more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months after inclusion.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Buying stocks because they just joined the index is more likely to lose than win over the following year.
insights.quote: there is evidence accumulating that companies that get added to the S&P 500 are more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months after the addition
insights.locator: Instead, as the value of funds indexed to the S&P 500 has surged

insights.claim: Tesla, which replaced Apartment Investment and Management (AIV) in the S&P 500 on December 18, 2020, underperformed the S&P 500 and massively underperformed the company it replaced in the months after inclusion.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The marquee inclusion case study refutes the bullish-inclusion narrative for high-profile additions.
insights.quote: Tesla not only under performed the S&P 500 in the months after its inclusion in the index, but massively underperformed the company (AIV) that it replaced in the index
insights.locator: I would recommend that you look at the chart from this study

insights.claim: The price trajectories of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will not be altered by whether they are included in or excluded from the S&P 500.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't position around index inclusion for these names; their fate hinges on the AI story, not index membership.
insights.quote: I don't think the price trajectories of any of these firms will be altered by whether they are included in or excluded from the S&P 500
insights.locator: I am sure that there will be some who will disagree with me

themes.theme: Passive investing vs momentum and market structure

insights.claim: Inclusion in a widely-tracked index increases intraday and short-term volatility but not price levels, because index-fund flows can reverse and momentum can cut both ways.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect index membership to raise a stock's volatility profile rather than create lasting price support.
insights.quote: inclusion in a widely-tracked index (like the S&P 500) will increase intraday and short term volatility, but not price levels
insights.locator: Momentum can cut in both directions

insights.claim: Top-heavy markets reflect winner-take-all economics rather than passive flows, because not all large-cap companies have been winners and the winning large caps delivered a disproportionate share of earnings growth.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Concentration is fundamentals-driven; betting on mean reversion against dominant winners ignores their earnings reality.
insights.quote: the winning large cap companies have delivered a disproportionate portion of increased earnings
insights.locator: Winner-take-all economics: If momentum is the reason

insights.claim: Most active investors and analysts are not seeking out original information or market inefficiencies but rely on publicly available information and a belief in mean reversion, so passive growth does little damage to genuine price discovery.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The 'passive kills price discovery' alarm overstates how much active research actually informs prices.
insights.quote: Much of active investing is built around publicly available information and a belief in the power of mean reversion, not original research and seeking information.
insights.locator: Active investing and equity research

insights.claim: Any endowment or pension fund manager who steers funds to active managers in service of preserving market efficiency, while accepting underperformance, would be in violation of fiduciary responsibility.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The market-efficiency justification for active fees cannot override the fiduciary duty to minimize cost drag.
insights.quote: any endowment or pension fund manager who uses this argument to steer endowment funds to active money managers would be in violation of his or her fiduciary responsibility
insights.locator: In fact, any endowment or pension fund manager

insights.claim: The 'passive makes markets inefficient' argument is hypocritical and self-serving because it is often advanced by the same fund managers who previously derided efficient markets as an academic fever dream.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Recognize the rhetorical flip as livelihood protection, possibly aimed at getting regulators to restrict index funds.
insights.quote: they also strike me as both hypocritical and self-serving
insights.locator: Since these stories are often being told by fund managers

insights.claim: An investor in an S&P 500 index fund is far less exposed to underperforming the market than a typical active manager who either over-invests in or avoids a name like SpaceX.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Diversified index exposure neutralizes single-name risk far better than concentrated active bets on volatile newcomers.
insights.quote: an investor in a S&P 500 index fund is far less exposed to underperforming the market than the typical active money manager who either over invests in SpaceX (if it goes down) or chooses not to invest in it (if it goes up)
insights.locator: Risk and Diversification: Is it true that individual investors

insights.claim: Institutional 'smart money' is more trader than investor, making them market followers rather than leaders, so the notion they can reliably time whether SpaceX is over- or undervalued is fanciful.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't assume institutions have a timing edge over retail; the smart-versus-stupid-money dichotomy is overdrawn.
insights.quote: institutional investors are more traders than investors, making them market followers, not leader
insights.locator: Smart and Stupid Money

insights.claim: An investor constrained to only money-making businesses over the last two decades would have underperformed an unconstrained investor even after adjusting for risk, because at the right price a money-losing company can be a good investment.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screening out unprofitable companies on principle has been a return drag; price, not current profitability, defines a good investment.
insights.quote: I will wager than an investor who was constrained to invest only in money-making businesses in the last two decades would have under performed an investor operating without those constraints, even after adjusting for risk.
insights.locator: Good businesses and good investments

themes.theme: Outlook for SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic

insights.claim: In the week before SpaceX's IPO, S&P confirmed it would keep its one-year-listed requirement, meaning SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic must wait a year after listing before index consideration.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: These names are off the index-inclusion table for at least a year post-IPO, removing any near-term inclusion catalyst.
insights.quote: S&P removed some of the suspense in the question of whether the company would be included in the index by announcing that they would stick with their requirement that a company be listed and traded at least a year
insights.locator: In the week prior to the SpaceX IPO

insights.claim: A year after listing, SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will still be money-losing businesses with works-in-progress business models and will remain corporate governance horror stories.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect continued losses, unproven models and weak governance from these names well past their first year public.
insights.quote: the truth is that a year after they list and start trading, these three companies will still be money losing businesses, with business models that are still works in progress and will remain corporate governance horror stories
insights.locator: The truth is that a year after they list

insights.claim: S&P needs these three trillion-dollar companies in its index more than they need to be in it, so any bending of inclusion rules will come from S&P, not the companies.
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch S&P for rule changes; a large-cap index that excludes the largest caps faces a credibility problem.
insights.quote: S&P needs these companies in its index more than they need to be in the index
insights.locator: The bottom line is that S&P needs these companies

insights.claim: The price paths of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will be determined by how the AI story plays out in both substance (growth, unit economics, reinvestment) and perception (hype and momentum).
insights.speaker: Posted by Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Track AI unit economics and reinvestment as the real drivers of these stocks rather than index or flow technicals.
insights.quote: their price paths will be determined by how the AI story continues to play out in terms of both substance (growth, unit economics, reinvestment) and perception (hype and momentum)
insights.locator: As for the companies (SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic)
